info.set_name: Cardiovascular Comprehensive Set 2: Regulation of Arterial Pressure (MCQ)

q1.prompt: A 16-year-old girl is diagnosed with Liddle's syndrome, a genetic mutation that increases the activity of epithelial sodium channels (ENaC). Which set of physiological findings is most consistent with the resulting effect on her arterial pressure regulation?

options.label: Increased plasma renin, increased aldosterone, and hyperkalemia
Decreased plasma renin, increased aldosterone, and metabolic acidosis
Increased plasma renin, decreased aldosterone, and hypernatremia
Decreased plasma renin, decreased aldosterone, and hypokalemia
Normal plasma renin, normal aldosterone, and hypotension

q2.prompt: A 2-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department after a head injury that resulted in increased intracranial pressure. Which analysis of his subsequent "Cushing reaction" correctly pairs the physiological stimulus with the cardiovascular outcome?

options.label: Decreased P_(CO2) in the medulla triggers reflex vasodilation and bradycardia
Increased P_(CO2) in the medulla triggers reflex vasoconstriction and tachycardia
Increased P_(CO2) in the medulla triggers intense sympathetic outflow, hypertension, and reflex bradycardia
Decreased P_(O2) in the carotid bodies triggers parasympathetic outflow, hypotension, and tachycardia
Increased pH in the cerebrospinal fluid triggers sympathetic inhibition and hypotension

q3.prompt: During a laboratory demonstration of the Valsalva maneuver, a student maintains forced expiration against a closed glottis. Which mechanical and reflex sequence explains the "rebound" bradycardia observed immediately after the student stops the maneuver?

options.label: Sudden decrease in intrathoracic pressure → decreased venous return → baroreceptor-mediated tachycardia
Sudden increase in cardiac output → decreased arterial pressure → decreased carotid sinus firing
Sudden increase in venous return → increased cardiac output → increased baroreceptor firing → reflex bradycardia
Persistent pulmonary hypertension → right ventricular failure → decreased heart rate
Rebound increase in total peripheral resistance → decreased mean systemic filling pressure → parasympathetic inhibition

q4.prompt: A 28-year-old woman experiences a 15% loss of total blood volume following an injury. Based on the baroreceptor reflex, which change in the firing rate of the carotid sinus nerve and subsequent autonomic outflow would be expected?

options.label: Increased firing rate → increased parasympathetic outflow → decreased heart rate
Decreased firing rate → increased sympathetic outflow → increased total peripheral resistance
Increased firing rate → decreased sympathetic outflow → increased venous compliance
Decreased firing rate → decreased parasympathetic outflow → decreased contractility
No change in firing rate → increased renin secretion → metabolic alkalosis

q5.prompt: A physiology researcher is comparing the effects of different hormones on a subject with expanded extracellular fluid (ECF) volume. Which of the following correctly describes the integrated response of Atrial Natriuretic Peptide (ANP) and Antidiuretic Hormone (ADH) to this state?

options.label: Decreased ANP secretion and increased ADH secretion to conserve water
Increased ANP secretion and increased ADH secretion to increase total peripheral resistance
Decreased ANP secretion and decreased ADH secretion to promote sodium reabsorption
Increased ANP secretion and decreased ADH secretion to promote natriuresis and water excretion
Increased ANP secretion and increased aldosterone secretion to stabilize blood volume

q6.prompt: A 65-year-old man with essential hypertension is prescribed an ACE inhibitor. Which of the following analytical shifts at the efferent arteriole is responsible for the reduction in his glomerular filtration rate (GFR)?

options.label: Constriction of the efferent arteriole leading to increased P_(GC)
Dilation of the efferent arteriole leading to decreased P_(GC)
Constriction of the afferent arteriole leading to increased RPF
Dilation of the afferent arteriole leading to decreased filtration fraction
Increased plasma protein concentration leading to increased π_(GC)

q7.prompt: Following a moderate hemorrhage, a patient's body utilizes "autotransfusion" to help restore blood volume. Which mechanism best describes how this shift of fluid from the interstitium into the capillaries is achieved?

options.label: Arteriolar vasodilation increases capillary hydrostatic pressure (P_c)
Increased plasma protein concentration increases interstitial oncotic pressure (π_i)
Arteriolar vasoconstriction decreases capillary hydrostatic pressure (P_c), favoring absorption
Increased venous pressure increases the net filtration pressure (J_v)
Decreased lymphatic flow increases the interstitial hydrostatic pressure (P_i)

q8.prompt: A patient is diagnosed with a tumor in the zona glomerulosa (Conn's syndrome) that causes primary hyperaldosteronism. Analyze the effect of this condition on the renin-angiotensin system and extracellular fluid volume.

options.label: High aldosterone → low Na^+ reabsorption → ECF volume contraction → high renin
High aldosterone → high Na^+ reabsorption → ECF volume expansion → low renin
High aldosterone → high Na^+ reabsorption → ECF volume expansion → high renin
High aldosterone → high K^+ reabsorption → ECF volume contraction → low renin
High aldosterone → low Na^+ reabsorption → ECF volume expansion → low renin

q9.prompt: During an acute drop in mean arterial pressure to 55 mmHg, which reflex mechanism becomes the primary driver for maintaining cerebral perfusion?

options.label: Peripheral chemoreceptors sensing decreased Pa_(O2) and increasing sympathetic outflow
Cardiopulmonary baroreceptors sensing decreased blood volume and increasing ADH
The Bainbridge reflex increasing heart rate to compensate for low stroke volume
The myogenic response of the aorta increasing total peripheral resistance
Atrial stretch receptors stimulating the release of ANP to lower afterload

q10.prompt: A 78-year-old patient has decreased arterial compliance due to aging. How will this change affect her systolic pressure and pulse pressure for a given stroke volume?

options.label: Decreased systolic pressure and decreased pulse pressure
Increased systolic pressure and decreased pulse pressure
Decreased systolic pressure and increased pulse pressure
Increased systolic pressure and increased pulse pressure
Normal systolic pressure and normal pulse pressure

q11.prompt: A patient with severe anemia (low hemoglobin) has a normal Pa_(O2) of 100 mmHg. Why are the peripheral chemoreceptors in the carotid bodies not stimulated in this patient?

options.label: Chemoreceptors are only sensitive to the total O_2 content of the blood
Chemoreceptors are only sensitive to the percentage of hemoglobin saturation
Chemoreceptors are primarily sensitive to dissolved PO_2, which is normal in anemia
The increased cardiac output in anemia washes out the stimulus
The Bohr effect prevents the chemoreceptors from reaching threshold

q12.prompt: A patient presenting with severe bradycardia (HR 35 bpm) is administered a therapeutic dose of atropine. Which application of the drug's mechanism explains the increase in heart rate?

options.label: Blockade of M_2 muscarinic receptors in the SA node, preventing G_i-mediated inhibition of adenylyl cyclase
Activation of β_1-adrenergic receptors, increasing intracellular cAMP levels
Inhibition of the Na^+/K^+ ATPase, increasing intracellular Ca^(2+)
Blockade of nicotinic receptors in the autonomic ganglia
Opening of K^+-ACh channels, leading to hyperpolarization

q13.prompt: A 48-year-old man moves from a supine to a standing position, resulting in a transient decrease in arterial pressure. Which of the following is the most immediate compensatory cardiovascular change?

options.label: Decreased total peripheral resistance
Increased venous compliance
Decreased firing rate of the carotid sinus nerve
Increased parasympathetic activity to the SA node
Decreased myocardial contractility

q14.prompt: Stimulation of the sympathetic nerves during exercise leads to an increase in blood glucose and fatty acids. Which analytical sequence describes the autonomic effect on the pancreatic islets?

options.label: Stimulation of insulin secretion and stimulation of glucagon secretion
Inhibition of insulin secretion and inhibition of glucagon secretion
Stimulation of insulin secretion and inhibition of glucagon secretion
Inhibition of insulin secretion and stimulation of glucagon secretion
Normalization of the insulin/glucagon ratio via the parasympathetic system

q15.prompt: In a healthy subject, an intravenous infusion of 2 liters of isotonic saline is administered. Which reflex is activated to increase heart rate?

options.label: Baroreceptor reflex
Cushing reflex
Hering-Breuer reflex
Bainbridge reflex
Chemoreceptor reflex

q16.prompt: During aerobic exercise, there is a redistribution of the cardiac output. Which analysis of regional blood flow explains the sparing of the cerebral circulation from sympathetic vasoconstriction?

options.label: High density of α_1 receptors on cerebral vessels causes strong dilation
Activation of β_2 receptors by epinephrine causes profound cerebral constriction
Local metabolic factors (e.g., CO_2) and autoregulation override sparse sympathetic innervation
The blood-brain barrier blocks norepinephrine from binding to cerebral smooth muscle
Increased parasympathetic outflow to the brain causes active vasodilation

q17.prompt: A patient with hypertension is treated with propranolol, a β-adrenergic antagonist. Which statement correctly analyzes the effect of this drug on his heart rate?

options.label: It increases heart rate by stimulating the "funny current" (I_f)
It decreases heart rate by decreasing the rate of Phase 4 depolarization in the SA node
It increases heart rate by decreasing the threshold potential
It decreases heart rate by hyperpolarizing the maximum diastolic potential
It has no effect on heart rate but decreases total peripheral resistance

q18.prompt: In response to a severe hemorrhage, antidiuretic hormone (ADH) levels increase significantly. Which set of receptors and outcomes represents the integrated role of ADH in this state?

options.label: V_1 receptors (water reabsorption) and V_2 receptors (vasoconstriction)
V_1 receptors (vasodilation) and V_2 receptors (sodium excretion)
V_1 receptors (sodium reabsorption) and V_2 receptors (water reabsorption)
V_1 receptors (vasoconstriction) and V_2 receptors (water reabsorption)
M_3 receptors (sweating) and V_2 receptors (thirst)

q19.prompt: A patient with Addison's disease experiences critical hypovolemia due to aldosterone deficiency. Why does their body exhibit a "hypotonic contraction" of the ECF?

options.label: Water shifts from the ECF to the ICF due to increased ECF osmolarity
Excessive water reabsorption in the proximal tubule dilutes the ECF
Severe hypovolemia resets the ADH threshold, leading to water retention despite low osmolarity
Hyperkalemia stimulates the thirst center directly, causing water intoxication
The loss of Na^+ is less than the loss of water, increasing the ECF osmolarity

q20.prompt: A clinician uses cocaine drops in a patient's eye during a test for Horner syndrome. If the sympathetic pathway to the eye is intact, what is the expected outcome and mechanism?

options.label: Constriction of the pupil due to blockade of norepinephrine release
Dilation of the pupil due to blockade of norepinephrine reuptake
Dilation of the pupil due to direct stimulation of muscarinic receptors
Constriction of the pupil due to activation of α_1 receptors
No change in pupil size because cocaine only affects the CNS

q21.prompt: Which of the following correctly pairs the stimulus with the renal response for the regulation of renin secretion?

options.label: Increased arterial pressure → increased renin release
Parasympathetic stimulation → increased renin release
Increased Cl^- concentration at the macula densa → increased renin release
Sympathetic stimulation of β_1 receptors on JG cells → increased renin release
Increased circulating angiotensin II → increased renin release

q22.prompt: A 54-year-old man presents with heatstroke after working in his yard on a hot day. Which analysis of his physiological state is correct regarding his sweating mechanism?

options.label: Sweating has ceased because high temperatures damaged the anterior hypothalamic-preoptic area
Sweating is excessive, leading to hypervolemic hypertension
Increased parasympathetic tone to the skin has caused profound vasoconstriction
The hypothalamic set point has been lowered, leading to shivering
ADH secretion is inhibited, causing polyuria and cooling of the core

q23.prompt: During a state of progressive hemorrhagic shock, a clinician administers low-dose dopamine. What is the intended analytical outcome of this therapy?

options.label: Global vasoconstriction to increase total peripheral resistance
Increased heart rate to compensate for low blood volume
Constriction of the renal artery to shunt blood to the brain
Dilation of the cutaneous vessels to dissipate heat
Dilation of renal and splanchnic arterioles to maintain blood flow to critical organs

q24.prompt: A patient with long-standing systemic hypertension is found to have decreased arterial compliance. According to the pressure-volume relationship, how will this affect the work of the heart?

options.label: It increases because the left ventricle must develop higher systolic pressures to eject blood
It decreases because the reduced capacitance facilitates faster ejection
It remains constant because the baroreceptor reflex lowers the heart rate
It decreases because the stroke volume is significantly reduced
It increases because the heart must pump blood into a higher-compliance system

q25.prompt: Thyroid hormones are known to have a "sympathomimetic" effect on the heart. What is the molecular application of this effect?

options.label: Inhibition of the Na^+/K^+ ATPase in myocardial cells
Down-regulation of muscarinic receptors in the SA node
Up-regulation of β_1-adrenergic receptors in the heart
Direct activation of the vagus nerve to increase cardiac reserve
Inhibition of PNMT in the adrenal medulla
